# Loredex dogfooding findings

## 2026-07-09 — DevOps lead: company-wide rollout of the loredex knowledge layer

Task: register all four Nimbus repos (ai-engine, backend, frontend, mobile) into one shared vault, wire the vault to a git remote, verify, and judge onboarding as if rolling out to 12 engineers.

### What I did

Ran `loredex init --vault <shared-vault> --project <name> --sync git` once per repo (four sequential runs, cd into each repo). Created a bare remote (`vault-remote.git`), added it as `origin` in the vault, and pushed. Verified with `loredex status`, `loredex doctor`, `loredex sync` (from nimbus-backend), and `loredex handoffs` (from nimbus-mobile). Committed the scaffolding files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .mcp.json, .cursor/rules/loredex.mdc) in each repo myself, since init leaves them untracked.

### What worked well

- `init` is fast and complete per-repo: one command created the vault once, registered each project, and dropped agent conventions (AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md + .cursor/rules) plus `.mcp.json` MCP wiring in one shot, with a clear output checklist.
- The vault is born as a git repo with an initial commit and a sensible `.gitignore` (excludes `.obsidian/workspace.json`, `.DS_Store`), so wiring a remote was just `remote add` + `push`.
- `loredex sync` is a nice one-word verb: commit local, pull, push — and it regenerated `_index/Dashboard.base` + `Home.md` as part of the sync commit. `handoffs` pulls the remote first, so an engineer sees teammates' handoffs without thinking about git.
- `doctor` is actionable: config path, vault, 4 registered projects, vault git repo, detected editors (Cursor, VS Code) and classifier CLIs (claude present, codex absent), flagged the unset editor.
- The dropped conventions are agent-aware: `.cursor/rules/loredex.mdc` explicitly bans ad-hoc SUMMARY.md/REPORT.md files and redirects agents to frontmattered notes — the right cultural lever.

### Friction / findings

1. **Vault remote wiring is entirely manual and invisible.** `init --sync git` creates the vault repo but nothing creates or wires a remote, and no command reports one missing. I had to `git init --bare`, `git remote add`, discover the branch name (`master`, from the machine's git default — half the company will get `main` and the first pull will fail), and push by hand. Doctor does not check "vault has a reachable remote / branch matches".
2. **Project registration is per-machine, not shared.** The 4-project registry lives in `config.json` under LOREDEX_CONFIG_DIR on my machine. The vault carries no record of which repos belong to it, so each of 12 engineers must re-run `init` in every repo, pointing at a vault path they must first clone themselves. No "join this vault" flow; no visibility into who registered what.
3. **Status vs doctor confusion.** `loredex status` from a repo shows `projects: 0` (vault note stats) while `doctor` says `projects registered 4`. Status only shows the current repo's unrouted candidates — no company-wide view of all repos, pending markdown, and open handoffs.
4. **gitattributes bug in the vault.** `init` writes `.git/info/attributes` with `Start\ Here\ -\ Product.md merge=loredex-generated`; git rejects backslash-escaped spaces there and prints `Here\ is not a valid attribute name` on operations like `git show`. The custom merge driver for "Start Here - Product.md" therefore will not apply — exactly the file most likely to conflict when 12 people sync.
5. **npx-vs-local inconsistency.** The generated `.mcp.json` runs `npx -y loredex@latest mcp`, and every hint (init "Next:" line, doctor's editor tip, the cursor rule) assumes the public npm package with default config. None of this inherits LOREDEX_CONFIG_DIR or a pinned version, so MCP-launched agents could operate against a different vault than the CLI the human uses.
6. **Init leaves repos dirty and silent about it.** Four untracked files per repo; nothing suggests committing them so teammates inherit the wiring. Also, `Home.md` links to `Dashboard.base` at init time, but the file only exists after the first sync/route — a dangling link in a fresh vault.
7. **Sync attribution comes from ambient git config.** The vault auto-commit used my personal global git identity; loredex never asks who you are, so vault history attribution across 12 engineers is whatever each machine happens to have.

### Desktop-app implications ("Obsidian replacement" lens)

- Onboarding wizard: create-or-join a vault (create remote in the same step), then batch-register local repos by scanning a folder — replacing 4x `cd && init` plus manual bare-repo/remote/push. Store a team identity for vault commits.
- Company overview home screen: all registered projects (from a shared registry stored inside the vault, not per-machine config), unrouted candidates per repo, open handoffs, last sync per teammate.
- Sync health surface: remote reachable, branch matches, last push/pull, merge-driver status — visible warnings instead of a buried gitattributes parse error.
- Team invites: a join link that encodes vault remote URL + branch + project registry so engineer #2..12 onboarding is "click, clone, done".

### GitHub / API-contract touchpoints observed

- The vault is itself a git repo; a hosted GitHub repo is the natural remote. A desktop app should create the private repo and wire origin during vault creation, and can render vault commit history ("who routed what") as a feed.
- Repo-side scaffolding needs to land on each repo's default branch to reach teammates — the app could open that PR per repo instead of leaving untracked files.
- nimbus-backend contains `openapi.yaml` + `postman_collection.json` (endpoint source of truth). Nothing in init/route knows they exist; contract ownership travels only by convention. The app should let a project declare "owned contract files" so endpoint-change handoffs link to the exact contract diff/commit.

### Onboarding verdict (12 engineers)

Per engineer today: install node/loredex, clone the vault, get the shared config right, run `init` once per repo (1-4 repos), commit the dropped files (first person per repo), and trust someone wired the remote correctly. Roughly 6-10 manual steps per person with two silent failure modes (missing remote, branch-name mismatch). The CLI core is solid; the multi-machine team layer (shared registry, remote wiring, identity, join flow) is the gap a desktop app should own.
